coredata.dc:title: Biofilms from poly-vinyl alcohol/palmyra root sprout with Boswellia serrata, carbon dots and anthocyanin for sensing the freshness of sardine fish
coredata.dc:creator: Koshy, Rekha Rose
K, Vishnu
Reghunadhan, Arunima
Mary, Siji K.
Koshy, Jijo Thomas
D, Sangeetha
Williams, Prakash G.
Pothan, Laly A.
coredata.dc:description: One of the main issues that customers worldwide have is food adulteration. In commercial packages, freshness cannot always be determined visually. Here, we propose sensitive films for use in food packaging that could alter colour to indicate a change in freshness. Hybrid, multifunctional, and eco-friendly films were prepared from polyvinyl alcohol/palmyra root sprout (PVA/PRS), fused with soy protein isolate carbon dot (CD), Boswellia serrata (BS), and Clitoriaternatea anthocyanin (CTE). The films showed pH sensitivity, antioxidant, and UV barrier properties. By creating hydrogen bonds between PRS and the other fillers, adding these substances makes PVA less crystallized. These interactions were verified by infrared Fourier-transform analysis. When compared to PVA, PRS films had significantly lower moisture content and swelling ratios. The UV-blocking capabilities of the films were greatly improved by the addition of CD, BS, and CTE without compromising their mechanical, thermal, or water vapor barrier properties. The composite film PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE exhibited a maximum tensile strength value of 69.47 ± 1.49 MPa. The CT extract provides the film with superior antioxidant properties. The colorimetric films PVA/PRS/CTE and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE showed distinct pH-responsive colour-change properties as well as good colour stability. The colorimetric films were used to test the freshness of sardine fish, and they revealed unique colour changes that indicated whether the fish sample was spoiled or not.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One of the main issues that customers worldwide have is food adulteration. In commercial packages, freshness cannot always be determined visually. Here, we propose sensitive films for use in food packaging that could alter colour to indicate a change in freshness. Hybrid, multifunctional, and eco-friendly films were prepared from polyvinyl alcohol/palmyra root sprout (PVA/PRS), fused with soy protein isolate carbon dot (CD), Boswellia serrata (BS), and Clitoriaternatea anthocyanin (CTE). The films showed pH sensitivity, antioxidant, and UV barrier properties. By creating hydrogen bonds between PRS and the other fillers, adding these substances makes PVA less crystallized. These interactions were verified by infrared Fourier-transform analysis. When compared to PVA, PRS films had significantly lower moisture content and swelling ratios. The UV-blocking capabilities of the films were greatly improved by the addition of CD, BS, and CTE without compromising their mechanical, thermal, or water vapor barrier properties. The composite film PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE exhibited a maximum tensile strength value of 69.47 ± 1.49 MPa. The CT extract provides the film with superior antioxidant properties. The colorimetric films PVA/PRS/CTE and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE showed distinct pH-responsive colour-change properties as well as good colour stability. The colorimetric films were used to test the freshness of sardine fish, and they revealed unique colour changes that indicated whether the fish sample was spoiled or not.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, efforts have been made to investigate sustainable packaging materials that are biodegradable and renewable due to concerns about environmental issues associated with non-degradable plastics and diminishing oil supplies. Environmentally friendly composite materials are being developed by numerous researchers worldwide for use in packaging and related fields. Several natural resources are being investigated in this area as a matrix for biopolymer films [1–3]. The underground sprout of the palmyra palm, known as the palmyra root sprout (PRS), is one such substance that can be utilized as a biopolymer matrix. The PRS powder exhibited great film-forming abilities and high mechanical properties when coupled with other nanomaterials or bioactive compounds. However, only a small number of researchers have examined the impact of using PRS as the polymer matrix. For the creation of bio-composites, PRS can be developed as a more advantageous substitute for petroleum-based polymer matrix because of its affordability and environmental friendliness.

A more serious effort needs to be made to accurately determine the shelf life of foods like fish and meat because eating spoiled food might have a negative impact on one's health. This goes beyond using renewable and sustainable food packaging. In this context, intelligent food packaging has received a lot of attention for determining the freshness of packaged food goods. A material with a component that monitors the state of packaged food throughout storage and transit is referred to as intelligent packaging. Additionally, it can serve as a barrier of protection, extending food's shelf life and minimizing food loss, as well as shielding it from external impacts. Consumers are typically informed about the freshness of packaged food using smart tags or other technologies. A novel advancement in intelligent packaging is pH indicator films that alter their colour to indicate food degradation. Anthocyanin, a pH-sensitive natural dye derived from plants, is frequently utilized to create pH-sensitive tags. The pigment anthocyanin belongs to the group of polyphenols called flavonoids, which impart vibrant hues to any plant tissue, such as flowers, roots, stems, fruits, and leaves [4]. Anthocyanin is ideal for producing intelligent packaging, since it is found to exhibit colour variations in different pH conditions. As food spoilage is often accompanied by pH changes, anthocyanin-rich films can be used to detect specific colour changes and monitor food freshness [5]. Among several sources of anthocyanins, Clitoria ternatea extract (CT) obtained from Clitoria ternatea flower has been extensively used as a natural source because of its strong colour and antioxidant properties.

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), a nontoxic polymer with outstanding film-forming capabilities as well as good tensile strength and flexibility [6–9], has been merged with PRS film to improve its qualities. However, the mechanical property fluctuations of PVA film brought on by humidity restrict its use. It has been found that zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CD) are very good at chemical stability, biocompatibility, and functionalization when added to this polymer matrix to make it stronger. In this study, carbon dots were created using soy protein isolate. The film has been strengthened with frankincense, also known technically as Boswellia serrata, in addition to CD to provide antioxidant action. Natural gum made from the Boswellia tree, frankincense, is said to offer several health benefits, including being anti-inflammatory, anti-bacterial, anti-fungal, and anti-tumour [10]. Due to its ability to inhibit the growth of the fungus Aspergillus, which causes food-damaging aflatoxin, it has been claimed to be an efficient food preservative. Biopolymer films have been reported by several authors to contain BS. Recently, Pirnia et al. [4] have reported the incorporation of frankincense in gelatin based films to improve their antioxidant activity. Reshmy et al. [11] have reported the incorporation of frankincense in gelatin based film to improve its antioxidant activity. Reshmy et al. [12] reinforced sugarcane bagasse derived nanocellulose with frankincense extract to impart an antimicrobial effect for controlling microbial growth for food packaging applications.

Therefore, a brand-new biocomposite film made of PVA/PRS was developed in this study using the solution casting approach and reinforced with CD, BS, and CTE extract. This is the first report on PRS-based biocomposite film formation. The effects of the addition of CD, BS, and CTE extracts on the physical and mechanical properties of PS alone and PVA/PRS films were studied. The prepared films were characterized using FTIR, XRD, AFM, and UV–visible spectroscopy. The antioxidant ability of film was studied to find the potential of PRS film to replace its overdependence on other biopolymer matrices. The applicability of the pH-sensitive film for monitoring the spoilage of sardine samples was also tested.

Powder made from palmyra root sprouts was manufactured by Soori organic palm firm, Ramanathapuram and purchased from a neighbourhood shop in Kerala's Alappuzha district. The PRS contains 26.04 % Carbon, 5.82 % hydrogen, 1.92 % Nitrogen and 0.20 % sulphur. The powder was made into a slurry by adding distilled water and stirring. For later usage, the filtrate was gathered and dried in a standard oven. PVA (polymerization degree (1750), purity ≥ 99 %) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The cosmetic grade Boswellia serrata (BS) was purchased online from BulkSupplements.com, 7511 Eastgate Rd, Henderson, USA and an ethyl acetate extract of BS was used. All other chemicals were of analytical grade.

Anthocyanin from Clitoria ternatea flower was extracted following the method adopted by Liu et al. [13] with some modifications. 100 g of fresh flowers were soaked in 500 mL of 80 % ethanol (Scheme I). The pH of the solution was adjusted to 2.0 with 1 M HCl, and the solution was kept at 4 °C for 24 h. After that, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min and filtered with Whatman 40 filter paper. Finally, the CTE was freeze dried for further use. The HPLC data of the extract is provided as Supplementary data S1.

Anthocyanin from Clitoria ternatea flower was extracted following the method adopted by Liu et al. [13] with some modifications. 100 g of fresh flowers were soaked in 500 mL of 80 % ethanol (Scheme I). The pH of the solution was adjusted to 2.0 with 1 M HCl, and the solution was kept at 4 °C for 24 h. After that, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min and filtered with Whatman 40 filter paper. Finally, the CTE was freeze dried for further use. The HPLC data of the extract is provided as Supplementary data S1.

The CD derived from SPI was prepared according to our previously reported method [14]. 2.5 g of SPI was mixed with 20 mL of water and the solution was made alkaline with 0.1 N NaOH. The mixture was heated in a Teflon equipped hydrothermal autoclave at 180 °C for 5 h After cooling the autoclave, the resulting brown solution was centrifuged at a high speed (10,000 rpm) for 15 min, followed by the filtration of an upper brown solution using a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The solution was then dried, and diluted with 50 mL of water, and stored at 4 °C (Scheme II).

The solution casting method was followed for the preparation of PVA/PRS films [15]. The film was prepared by heating 1 g of PVA and 10 % glycerol (based on the total weight of PVA and PRS) in 100 mL of distilled water, followed by the addition of 2 g of palmyra root sprout powder. The blended solution was subjected to magnetic stirring at 80 °C for 1 h. The control film solution was poured into a Teflon coated pan (13 × 27 cm) and dried in a vacuum oven overnight at 55 °C.

PVA/PRS/CD composite films, PVA/PRS/BS films and PVA/PRS/CTE films were prepared by adding a previously sonicated 5 % of CD, 5 % Boswellia serrata powder and 5 % of CTE extract separately to the PVA/PRS blend solution followed by magnetic stirring at 80 °C for 1 h. The final solution was cast and dried as previously.
Finally, PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE composite films were prepared by adding 5 % CD, 5%BS and 5 % CTE to the blend solution of PVA/PRS, followed by magnetic stirring at 80 °C for 1 h and oven drying at 55 °C.

Neat PVA films were prepared by dissolving 1 g of PVA in 100 mL distilled water and neat PRS films, by dissolved 2 g of PRS 100 mL distilled water followed by magnetic stirring at 80 °C for 1 h. Both solutions were cast and dried in overnight under the previously mentioned conditions.

The colour parameters of the film sample were determined by the SC-80C colourimeter and its (CIE L, a, b values, (L0(lightness), a0 (redness to greenness), b0 (yellowness to blueness)) were determined. L0can have values from 0 (black) to 100 (pure white), a0 can have values from negative (green) to positive (red) and b0 can assume values from negative (blue) to positive (yellow). Whiteness Index (WI) and Yellowness Index (YI) were measured. All measurements of the samples were taken in triplicate at different locations. The results were reported as the means of measurements.

An atomic force microscope (AFM, NX10, Park System) was used to analyze the surface morphology of the composite films. Measurements were taken from several areas of the film surface (50 × 50 μm and 5 × 5 μm) using the tapping mode. Average roughness (Ra), root-mean-square roughness (Rq) were calculated.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of the samples was obtained using a Perkin Elmer Model 1000 FTIR spectrometer fitted with an ATR accessory. IR spectra were scanned within the range of 4000–500 cm−1.

The UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to measure the UV and visible light transmittance of the samples at 200–800 nm after the film was carved into thin strips of dimension 2 cm × 2 cm.

Using Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation running at 40 kV and 30 mA, an X-ray diffractometer D5000 apparatus made by Siemens was used to get the XRD of the composite films. Radiation scattered in the range of 5°–70° was observed.

With an accelerating voltage of 20 kV, the surface morphology of the composite films was examined with a Carl Zeiss Scanning Electron Microscope. The cross-sectional pictures were captured to verify the inclusions.

A thermogravimetric analyzer (Shimadzu TGA-Q500 equipment) was used to assess the thermal stability of the film samples. A sample weighing around 5–6 mg was heated at 10 °C/min under nitrogen atmosphere (50 mL/min) at intervals of 20–900 °C.

A micrometer (Mitutoyo 7301, Japan) was used to measure the thickness of the films with an accuracy of 0.01 mm. For each film, three randomized measurements were made, and the average values were calculated.

Tensile characteristics of each film, including elongation at break percentage (E) and tensile strength (TS), was measured using an Instron Universal Testing Machine -Model 5565, in accordance with the standard procedure of ASTM D 882-88. The film sample was cut into rectangular strips of 2.5 cm × 15 cm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture content, swelling degree and water solubility
ce:section.ce:para:
The moisture content of composite films was measured using a digital moisture analyzer (Wensar, PGB, 1MB). Triplicate measurements were done. The degree of swelling was monitored following the method of Gürler et al. [16] with slight modifications. Triplicate measurements were done [17]. Degree of swelling (SD) and water solubility (WS) were calculated as

W1- constant weight attained for the films after drying at 105 °C in an oven for 24 h
W2- weight measured after immersing them in 50 mL distilled water for 2 h then dried with filter paper.
W3- Constant weight of the films obtained after drying again at 105 °C in an oven
The Water Vapor Permeability (WVP) of the films was measured gravimetrically using the ASTM standard test procedure as previously reported by us [14].

The WVP was calculated by the following equation:
(5)
WVP =
W × X
t × A × ΔP
where W was the gain in weight of bottle (g), X was film thickness (m), t was time (s), A was the area of the film sample (m2) and ΔP was saturated vapor pressure at 25 °C.

The antioxidant activity of the composite films was tested using DPPH assays. The detailed procedure is already reported by us [14]. DPPH radical scavenging activity (%) was measured using the formula

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH sensing ability of indicator films
ce:section.ce:para: The pH-sensing ability of film samples containing anthocyanin was determined by the method of Tavassoli et al. [18]. For this, the film sample was immersed in different buffer solutions (pH 1.0–11.0) for 5 min. Colour parameters L*, a*, b* were recorded using SC-80C colourimeter.

ΔE was calculated using the formula [19]

where L0, a0, b0 are the colour parameters of the original films. In addition, to confirm colour stability, the films were stored at room temperature for six months and the experiment was repeated [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of films for monitoring sardine freshness
ce:section.ce:para: The colorimetric films, PVA/PRS/CTE and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE were applied to monitor the freshness of sardines during storage. 30 g of fresh sardine was placed in a sealed box attached with a colour label. The cut film was placed at the central space of the label making sure that there is no direct contact with the fish samples. A digital pH meter was used to monitor the pH variations in the fish sample. The box was stored at 25 °C for 3 days and colour changes were monitored. During this time, their total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N), was also measured, accompanied by photographing the smart films. During sampling, each process was repeated three times.

Film samples measuring 1 × 1 cm2 were shaped into squares before being sterilised for 1 h under ultraviolet light. S. aureus, E. coli, and S. pyogenes were identified using the agar disc diffusion method. A LB agar plate was saturated with 100 μL of a bacterial suspension containing approximately 1 × 106 CFU/mL. The sterile samples were placed with care in the centre of the plates. Zones of inhibition were determined following a 24-hour incubation period at a temperature of 37 °C. As the positive control, gentamycin was utilized, while distilled water served as the negative control. Following this, the inhibition zone diameters were assessed using a digital Vernier calliper, and the mean diameter (in millimetres) was calculated. Each experiment was conducted in triplicate.

Each film was measured in triplicate and the results were expressed as the average values ± standard deviation. A significant difference (p < 0.05) was determined using a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and Duncan's multiple range test.

The appearance of the films is shown in Fig. 1 . The PVA film was highly transparent and had the highest L (lightness) value (82.71 ± 0.69) and WI (whiteness index) (81.22 ± 0.66). The PRS film displayed a slight yellowish appearance. The PVA/PRS blend exhibited good film forming properties, which can be attributed to the formation of inter- and intramolecular hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl groups on the backbone of the two components. The L value of PVA films decreased with the addition of PRS. PVA/PRS films had an L value of 64.18 ± 0.65. However, the a and b values drastically increased with the addition of PRS. This result indicates that the lightness of the film decreased with increased redness and yellowness after blending with PRS. In fact, PRS, PVA/PRS, and PVA/PRS/CD films had a high b value, indicating a yellow colour. The high yellowness index of these three films also indicated a yellow colour. PVA/PRS/CD film had a high a value due to its light brown colour. While the separate addition of CD and BS in PVA/PRS did not produce drastic colour differences, the addition of CTE led to a grape purple colour. The composite PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE film had a purple colour. When compared to PVA/PRS films, PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films had the lowest L, a, and b values, WI, and YI. The colour properties of films were evaluated and are presented in Table 1 .

The atomic force microscopy (AFM) method was used to study the surface topography of film materials. Fig. 2(a-i to g-i) is the two-dimensional surface topography of PVA (a-i), PRS (b-i), PVA/PRS (c-i), PVA/PRS/CD (d-i), PVA/PRS/BS (e-i), PVA/PRS/CTE (f-i), and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE (g-i) films. It was seen that all of the film matrices had structural surfaces that were mostly continuous and good. This shows that the fillers were firmly mixed into the composite films. Fig. 2(a-ii-g-ii) represents the three-dimensional surface topography of PVA (a-ii), PRS (b-ii), PVA/PRS (c-ii), PVA/PRS/CD (d-ii), PVA/PRS/BS (e-ii), PVA/PRS/CTE (f-ii), and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE (g-ii). The three-dimensional surface topography clearly shows the interaction between the matrices and the fillers with the matrices. Fig. 2(a-iii-g-iii) shows the graphical representation of the surface topographical values of PVA (a-iii), PRS (b-iii), PVA/PRS (c-iii), PVA/PRS/CD (d-iii), PVA/PRS/BS (e-iii), PVA/PRS/CTE (f-iii), and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE (g-iii). The graph shows that as the peak intensity increases, the roughness values also increase. PVA film had the lowest surface roughness values of Sa 8.24 nm and Sq 10.44 nm, revealing that PVA exhibited a plain and homogeneous surface morphology. The surface roughness of PRS and PVA/PRS films was very high compared to PVA. The surface roughness of PVA/PRS films decreased (though not less than PVA) with the addition of fillers, proving that the fillers were distributed evenly in the film matrices. However, the surface roughness of PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films was the highest (Sa 145.49 nm and Sq 177.99 nm). This may be attributed to the partial agglomeration and sedimentation of the fillers during the drying process. Partial agglomeration along with the strong intermolecular forces of the fillers with the matrices were the main reasons for the increase in the surface roughness of the films [21]. Erbil et al. [22] have reported a positive correlation with the surface roughness and hydrophobicity showing that the larger the surface roughness, the greater is its hydrophobicity. This can be attributed to the fact that the roughness increases the fraction of air entrapped on the surface and thereby increases the water contact angle and hydrophobicity. The hydrophobic nature of PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films when compared to PVA films, which is discussed in the later portion of this paper, can be correlated with the increased surface roughness value of PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films.

The UV–visible light barrier property is an essential criterion to be monitored for food packaging films since the absorption of UV light can lead to the oxidation of food products and subsequent nutrient loss. In the presence of UV light, cells produce reactive oxygen species, which can lead to lipid peroxidation of the cell membrane, resulting in cell damage and thus accelerating food spoilage [23]. UV–vis transmittance and absorbance spectra of the films are shown in Fig. 3(a & b). Neat PVA film showed the maximum transmittance in the visible region, while the transmittance of neat PRS films and other composite films was less. Neat PVA films showed a characteristic absorption at 194 nm, which can be attributed to the existence of the carbonyl groups CO and CC of PVA [24], indicating that PVA still had a specific UV shielding function. Compared with PVA, PRS and all the other composite films showed good UV blocking ability in UVB (280–315 nm) and UVC (220–280 nm) regions. In fact, the T280 of PRS films was 99.7 % less than PVA films.

PRS is found to contain alkaloids, saponins, flavonoids, glycosides, coumarins, phenols, tannins, and quinines which have unique UV absorption properties [25]. The UV absorption of CD incorporated film can be attributed to the characteristic UV absorption of CD at 365 nm [26]. The UV absorption of PVA/PRS/BS films can be attributed to the presence of aromatic –OH group in BS, which enhanced the n − π∗ absorbance in the range 200–400 nm. Narasagoudr et al. [27] have cited a similar UV absorption of chitosan/poly (vinyl alcohol) films on the addition of boswellic acid. The UV absorption of PVA/PRS/CTE is due to the presence of phenolic compounds and anthocyanin in the structure of CTE. The composite film PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE was found to absorb both UVA, UVB and UVC regions. This high UV blocking ability is very important for increasing the shelf life of UV sensitive food materials and drugs. These films also have promising roles in UV-blocker nanocomposites or coatings [28].

On the other hand, with the addition of fillers, the transparency of the films was found to decrease in agreement with the colour change. This decrease can be attributed to the scattering of light by the fillers present in the matrix [29]. T660 of PRS film, was 55.95 % less than PVA films, and T660 of PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films was 88.18 % less than PVA films. These results indicate that the addition of fillers increases the UV blocking properties of the film at the expense of less transparency. Kim et al. [20] have also observed a similar decrease in transparency of gelatin/agar-based colour-indicator film integrated with Clitoriaternatea flower anthocyanin and zinc oxide nanoparticles.

The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3(c)) was analyzed to get an idea of the structure and interactions happening in the composite films. The spectra of PVA shows bands at 3300, 2941,1418,1325,1107,1037, 916 cm−1corresponding to OH stretching, CH asymmetric stretching, CHCH2 bending, δ(OH)rocking with CH wagging, shoulder stretching of CO, CO stretching in basic carbon skeleton, CH2 rocking respectively. PRS shows a broad band in the range 3100-3400 cm−1 corresponding to X-H stretch (X is O, N), indicating the presence of hydroxyl group, or imides. Peaks at 2928 cm−1 correspond to CH stretching vibration of alkyl molecules in aliphatic bonds. The peak around 1156 cm−1–1080 cm−1 is ascribed to the CO stretching and CH deformation vibrations. Compared to the spectra of neat PVA and neat PRS films, the spectra of composite films showed a slight shift in the wavenumber for all the peaks and this can be attributed to the intermolecular interactions happening between the components. Specifically, the band corresponding to the OH bending of absorbed water which occurs at 1639 cm−1 in neat PVA and neat PRS film shift to higher wavenumbers in composite films, which can be attributed to the interaction between bound water and carbonyl bonds [30]. Furthermore, the formation of a new peak at 1090 cm−1 in PVA/PRS, PVA/PRS/CD, PVA/PRS/BS, PVA/PRS/CTE, PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE composite films shows the formation of hydrogen bonds with film components and added fillers. The absence of characteristic peaks of the CD at 1589 cm−1 [26], BS at 1720 cm−1 owing to C = O group [31], CTE at 1638 cm−1 due to CC vibration in aromatic ring [18] also showed the interaction (electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bond) between the fillers and the matrices. The FTIR spectra of the added components are provided as Supplementary data S2.

The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3(c)) was analyzed to get an idea of the structure and interactions happening in the composite films. The spectra of PVA shows bands at 3300, 2941,1418,1325,1107,1037, 916 cm−1corresponding to OH stretching, CH asymmetric stretching, CHCH2 bending, δ(OH)rocking with CH wagging, shoulder stretching of CO, CO stretching in basic carbon skeleton, CH2 rocking respectively. PRS shows a broad band in the range 3100-3400 cm−1 corresponding to X-H stretch (X is O, N), indicating the presence of hydroxyl group, or imides. Peaks at 2928 cm−1 correspond to CH stretching vibration of alkyl molecules in aliphatic bonds. The peak around 1156 cm−1–1080 cm−1 is ascribed to the CO stretching and CH deformation vibrations. Compared to the spectra of neat PVA and neat PRS films, the spectra of composite films showed a slight shift in the wavenumber for all the peaks and this can be attributed to the intermolecular interactions happening between the components. Specifically, the band corresponding to the OH bending of absorbed water which occurs at 1639 cm−1 in neat PVA and neat PRS film shift to higher wavenumbers in composite films, which can be attributed to the interaction between bound water and carbonyl bonds [30]. Furthermore, the formation of a new peak at 1090 cm−1 in PVA/PRS, PVA/PRS/CD, PVA/PRS/BS, PVA/PRS/CTE, PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE composite films shows the formation of hydrogen bonds with film components and added fillers. The absence of characteristic peaks of the CD at 1589 cm−1 [26], BS at 1720 cm−1 owing to C = O group [31], CTE at 1638 cm−1 due to CC vibration in aromatic ring [18] also showed the interaction (electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bond) between the fillers and the matrices. The FTIR spectra of the added components are provided as Supplementary data S2.

The crystalline/amorphous nature of the prepared film samples were analyzed using XRD (Fig. 3(d)). For neat PVA film, a strong diffraction peak was observed at 19.7° (101) [32]. PRS shows characteristic peaks at 17.06° and 22.10°characteristics of B-type crystalline starch molecules [33]. The addition of PRS to PVA decreased the crystallinity of PVA which confirms the formation of hydrogen bonds between PVA and PRS which destroyed the original arrangement of PVA and PRS. Kang et al. [34] also observed a similar decrease in crystallinity of PVA matrix when okra mucilage was added to it. The peak intensity of PVA/PRS films at 2Ɵ19.7° was weakened with the addition of CD, BS, CTE indicating the amorphous state and good miscibility of the fillers in the matrices and the result was consistent with the reports of Ahmadi et al. [18]. The peak intensity further decreased in PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE composite film due to formation of more hydrogen bonds between PAV/PRS and the fillers [35].

The microstructure of the composite films PVA/PRS and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE, were characterized using SEM analysis as shown in Fig. 4 . The cross section of PVA/PS films was rough and cranny while the cross-section of the PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE film were homogenous, compact and smooth without any cracks or holes. The smooth surface of PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films shows homogenous distribution of fillers in the matrices due to the formation of hydrogen bonding. Also, the folds in the PVA/PRS films decreased when extract was added as extracts could fill the gap by forming new hydrogen bonds with PVA/PRS polymer matrix. Liu et al., [35] also reported similar phenomenon in purple potato incorporated chitosan/polyvinyl alcohol/nano-ZnO matrix.

The thermal stability of the PVA/PRS-based films was evaluated using thermogravimetric analysis, and the resulting TGA and DTG curves were shown in Fig. 5(a & b). Multi-step weight loss was observed for all the films. The first weight change resulting from evaporation of loosely bound water molecules was observed at 58–91 °C. The second degradation step varied depending on the type of film and fillers and was observed between 114 °C and 240 °C, which were attributed to the thermal decomposition of glycerol. According to the previous reports [36], the thermal degradation of pure PVA films occurred in two steps, the first one at around 80 °C and the second one at 200 °C. The third decomposition occurred at 242–330 °C for PRS films, 248–328 °C for PVA/PRS/BS, PVA/PRS/CTE and at 247–357 °C for PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films. For PVA/PRS and PVA/PRS/CD films, the third decomposition occurred at 258–388 °C and 258–374 °C respectively. A final degradation step between 410 and 460 °C was observed for all films except PRS which shows the enhanced thermal stability obtained due to synergistic effect of PVA and PRS. The temperature at 50 % weight loss T50 was 295.58, 275.61, 298.51, 288.00, 307.97 and 313.96 °C for PRS, PVA/PRS, PVA/PRS/CD, PVA/PRS/BS, PVA/PRS/CTE and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films respectively. T50 of PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films were 18 °C higher than PRS films. The residual mass percentage was 16.00 %, 2.92 %, 3.08 %, 10.49 %, 16.16 % and 12.66 % for PRS, PVA/PRS, PVA/PRS/CD, PVA/PRS/BS, PVA/PRS/CTE and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films, respectively. The films with CTE had more terminal residue than films without CTE at 800 °C. This can be attributed to the presence of polyacylated anthocyanins known as “ternatins”, which are polyacylated derivatives of delphinidin 3,3′,5′-triglucoside. Guo et al. [23] have also shown that the thermal stability of gelatin films were enhanced on the addition of litchi extract.

The antioxidant activity of the composite films was monitored using a DPPH assay (Fig. 5(c)). The control PVA film showed no DPPH radical scavenging activity while the control PRS film showed maximum activity of 30 %. When compared to the control PRS film all other films showed less scavenging activity. In PVA/PRS films, the entanglement between PVA and PRS made the active groups in PRS ineffective, decreasing the antioxidant activity of the films. In PVA/PRS/CD films, the hydrogen bonding between hydroxyl groups on PVA/PRS molecular chains and hydroxyl groups of CDs made the active groups ineffective. Also, the solubility of the film decreased owing to increased crosslinking. The fact that CD from SPI does not have inherent antioxidant activity coherently contributed to a low value. When compared to the addition of CD, on adding BS to PVA/PRS films, the antioxidant values slightly increased owing to the antioxidant potential of BS [37]. On adding CTE to PVA/PRS films, the antioxidant activity further increased to 15 % demonstrating that CTE has excellent antioxidant properties. Ratha et al. [38] found that CTE contains gallic acid, sinapinic acid, chlorogenic acid, p-coumaric acid and ferulic acid which have good antioxidant potential. Grzebieniarz et al. [39] have also shown that CT contains abundant gallic acid which has led to good antioxidant activity of furcellaran double-layered films. However, the DPPH radical scavenging activity of CTE was not as pronounced in the film as expected [20]. This can lead to the assumption that not all polyphenols were extracted sufficiently whose presence is necessary for the scavenging activity. Kurek et al. [40] also observed that there was not much increase in the antioxidant activity of chitosan film with the increased addition of anthocyanin extracts due to the decrease insolubility of the composite film. In PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE composite films, the free radical scavenging rate was slightly less than PRS. This can be attributed to the fact that excess CTE accumulated on the surface of the film which not only reduced the number of active hydroxyl groups but also through the formation of hydrogen bonds and esterification reactions, the availability of free hydroxyl groups was drastically reduced resulting in decreased free radical scavenging activity. Guo et al. [23] have also observed a similar trend when litchi shell extract was added to gelatin/chitosan/PVA films when compared to gelatin/chitosan films.

The thickness, tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) values of the films are given in Table 2 . PRS film exhibited a thickness value of 0.15 ± 0.01.PVA/PRS films exhibited slightly higher thickness value when compared to PVA and PRS films. The addition of the CD was found to decrease the thickness of PVA/PRS films significantly (p < 0.05). However, there was not significant (p > 0.05) difference on adding BS and CTE. However, the composite film PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films had significantly (p < 0.05) higher thickness value.

Neat PVA film exhibited a tensile strength (TS) of 28.25 ± 1.65 MPa and the elongation at break value (EB) 174.14 ± 3.84 %. PRS film displayed a TS value of 26.63 ± 1.27 MPa and an EB of 28.42 ± 3.22 %. The combined PVA/PRS film exhibited a good EB of 165.91 ± 4.01 %. PRS interacted with the polyhydroxy groups of PVA through hydrogen bonding which in turn strengthened the network structure of the film enhancing the density and flexibility of the films and thereby increasing the EB. Guo et al. [23] have also reported that the addition of PVA to gelatin chitosan matrix increased its EB value. However, with the addition of CD and BS, the EB drastically decreased to 7.06 ± 2.95 % and 4.88 ± 2.98 % respectively and increased the TS to 49.74 ± 2.27 MPa and 51.19 ± 2.03 MPa respectively, which shows the formation of crosslinking of CD and BS with the PVA/PRS matrix which restrict the mobility of the polymer chains. Reshmy et al. [10] have also reported a decrease in EB of PVA/cellulose film on addition of BS. This can be attributed to the lower mobility of the matrices on addition of BS. When compared to CD and BS, the addition of CTE slightly decreased the TS to 23.15 ± 2.02 MPa and increased the EB to 32.08 ± 3.06 % which can be attributed to the plasticizing effect of the phenolic compounds present in anthocyanin [41]. CTE contains many organic components with bulk spatial structures which result in the loose structure of films thereby decreasing the TS. It is also well known that anthocyanin can act as plasticizer thereby breaking the secondary bonds and increasing the polymer chain mobility. Similar reductions in TS have been observed in various other composite films having natural anthocyanins [42]. The composite film PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE exhibited the maximum tensile strength value of 69.47 ± 1.49 MPa. The improved mechanical property was attributed to good compatibility and intermolecular interactions among PVA, PRS, CD, BS and CTE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture content, swelling ability and water solubility of the films
ce:section.ce:para: Moisture content (MC) (Table 2) was highest in PVA film due to its hydrophilic nature [35]. PRS film showed MC value of 18.69 % which was in agreement with the findings of Sobini et al. [43]. Compared to PVA and PRS films, PVA/PRS film showed significantly less (p < 0.05) MC which can be believed to be due to the presence of PRS. On adding CD to PVA/PRS matrices, the MC significantly (p < 0.05) decreased. The CD contains functional groups like -OH, -NH2, -COOH on its surface which can form hydrogen bonds with the matrices inhibiting the availability of free groups to form hydrogen bonding with water molecules and thus reduce the moisture content [44]. Ezati et al. [45] have also shown that addition of sulphur functionalized carbon dots reduced the MC of pectin/gelatine based films. The addition of BS to PVA/PRS films was also found to decrease (p > 0.05) MC value. BS contains -OH/-COOH functional groups which can similarly interact with the matrices [10]. When compared to the addition of CD and BS, addition of CTE was found to slightly increase the MC. This slight increase can be attributed to the inherent hydrophilic nature of CTE [46]. The final PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films possessed an MC value of 13.63 ± 0.56, which was significantly lower (p < 0.05) than the MC of PVA, PRS and PVA/PRS films. The total void space available for water molecules inside the films decreased with the addition of fillers [47]. Overall, the strong interactions between the polymers, fillers and reduction in void space causes a reduction in water absorption by functional groups of biopolymers resulting in higher resistance to moisture content.

Swelling ability (SD) is an important criteria which should be monitored for the development of indicator films as it has an effect on the colour response efficiency of the indicator label [48]. SD indicates the total water molecules occupying the microstructure. The SD of the composite films are shown in Table. From the table it is clear that as compared with neat PVA films (SD = 62.95 ± 0.99), neat PRS films exhibited significantly less (p < 0.05) SD (36.96 ± 1.4). The SD of PVA/PRS films was 58.90 ± 1.58. The addition of fillers significantly (p < 0.05) reduced the SD of PVA/PRS films. The presence of fillers in the matrices causes intermolecular interaction thereby diminishing the availability of free hydroxyl groups in the matrices for bonding with water molecules [49].

Water solubility (WS) (Table 2) of PVA/PRS films was significantly less (p < 0.05) than PVA and PRS control films. The addition of CD and BS to PVA/PRS films was found to significantly decrease the WS. On the other hand, the addition of CTE was found to significantly increase the WS. This increase can be attributed to the hydrophilic groups of the aqueous Clitoria extract, which improve interactions between the biopolymer matrix and water [39].

The water barrier property of the film was measured by calculating its WVP (Table 2). It measures the ability of the film to prevent moisture transfer [50]. PVA films possessed a WVP value of 4.60 ± 0.85 × 10−11 g−1 s−1 Pa−1. The WVP of PRS films were slightly less but not significantly less (p > 0.05) from PVA films. PVA/PRS film possessed WVP of 2.77 ± 0.70 × 10−11 g−1 s−1 Pa−1.Separate additions of CD and BS did not significantly (p > 0.05) decrease the WVP of PVA/PRS films. However, the addition of CTE to PVA/PRS films was found to significantly increase the WVP. This is due to the presence of hydrophilic anthocyanin in CTE. Furthermore, the immobilization of phenolic compounds in the biopolymer matrices can change the spatial arrangement of polymeric chains of biopolymers resulting in an easier transportation path of water vapor in the film. Santos et al. [51] have also shown that the incorporation of CTE increased the WVP of alginate films. Guo et al. [23] also found that the addition of anthocyanin-rich litchi extract in gelatin/chitosan/PVA matrix increased its WVP. Riahi et al. [52] also observed a similar increase in WVP of gelatin film when grapeseed extract was added to it. PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films had the lowest WVP among the seven films which can be ascribed to the compact and dense networks formed through intermolecular interactions between matrices and the fillers. Moreover, the fillers act as crosslinking agents bringing PVA and PRS polymer chains closer to each other thereby creating a circuitous pathway for water vapor movement in the film [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colour response of PVA/PRS/CTE and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films to different pH buffers
ce:section.ce:para:
The visual colour and colour parameters (L*, a*, b* and ΔE) of PVA/PRS/CTE and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films in different pH buffer solutions are shown in Tables 3 & 4
. Both the films are sensitive to pH and showed a visible colour change in the pH range of 1–11. As expected for CTE immobilized films, PVA/PRS/CTE and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films exhibited reddish pink at pH 1–3, purple at pH 4–7, cyan at pH 8–11 [53]. All the films exhibited high L* value indicating high brightness. Both the films showed high a* value at low pH (1–3) and hence red colour. The a* (redness) value decreased with increasing pH which indicates that when the films changed from redness to greenness [20]. A significant decrease (p < 0.05) in b (blueness/yellowness) values was also observed the films converted to dark blue in alkaline pH. The colour changes at different pH were ascribed to the structural transformation of anthocyanin occurring at different pH. The flavylium cation in acidic conditions becomes carbinol pseudo base or chalcone in neutral condition and shifts to anionic quinoidal when the pH changes to alkalinity [54]. For both the films, the ΔE values were above 3 which shows that the colour changes were visibly perceivable [35]. Compared to PVA/PRS/CTE films, PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films showed darker and distinctive colour changes with change in pH. Similar colour changes were observed when CTE flower anthocyanin and zinc oxide nanoparticles were incorporated in gelatine/agar-based colour-indicator films [20].

The colour stability of indicator films is an essential feature to be monitored as it determines the efficiency and quality of smart packages. The colour stability of PVA/PRS/CTE and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films were tested after storage for six months and it was found that though the colour faded slightly after six months, both the films showed clear colour changes with pH. The good colour stability of the colorimetric films may be due to the fact that both PVA and PRS may entrap the anthocyanin and prevent it from getting oxidized thus preserving the colour. Zhai et al., [55] have also reported the ability of PVA and starch in preserving the colour of roselle anthocyanins.

The antimicrobial activity of PVA, PRS, PVA/PRS and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films were tested against E. coli, S. aureus and S. pyogenes bacteria. The images obtained during the antimicrobial activity are presented in Fig. 6. Pure PVA did not show inhibition zone against any bacteria indicating that PVA had no antibacterial activity, a similar result was observed by other researchers [27]. Pure PRS film showed trace activity against S. pyogenes but no activity against E. coli and S. aureus. Arirudran et al. [56] have reported the same activity results with same bacterial species on PRS films. Since the usage of only 5 wt% palmyra root sprout powder in PRS films, it did not enable us to observe antibacterial activity in the film. PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films showed slight antibacterial activity against S. aureus and S. pyogenes but no activity against E. coli. Low antibacterial activity of the films as against the expected results may be compared to the reported work of Tummalapalli et al. [57] wherein they have reported null antibacterial activity of composite wound dressings of pectin and gelatine with Aloe vera and curcumin as bioactive agents. The table of the results is provided as Supplementary data S3. The reduction in antibacterial activity of the films is in correlation with the reduced antioxidant activity which in turn confirms the good interaction of the fillers with the matrices which prevents the leaching out of the bioactive compounds from the matrices.

The antimicrobial activity of PVA, PRS, PVA/PRS and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films were tested against E. coli, S. aureus and S. pyogenes bacteria. The images obtained during the antimicrobial activity are presented in Fig. 6. Pure PVA did not show inhibition zone against any bacteria indicating that PVA had no antibacterial activity, a similar result was observed by other researchers [27]. Pure PRS film showed trace activity against S. pyogenes but no activity against E. coli and S. aureus. Arirudran et al. [56] have reported the same activity results with same bacterial species on PRS films. Since the usage of only 5 wt% palmyra root sprout powder in PRS films, it did not enable us to observe antibacterial activity in the film. PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films showed slight antibacterial activity against S. aureus and S. pyogenes but no activity against E. coli. Low antibacterial activity of the films as against the expected results may be compared to the reported work of Tummalapalli et al. [57] wherein they have reported null antibacterial activity of composite wound dressings of pectin and gelatine with Aloe vera and curcumin as bioactive agents. The table of the results is provided as Supplementary data S3. The reduction in antibacterial activity of the films is in correlation with the reduced antioxidant activity which in turn confirms the good interaction of the fillers with the matrices which prevents the leaching out of the bioactive compounds from the matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of colorimetric films in monitoring the freshness of sardines
ce:section.ce:para: Microbial activity is the main reason for fish spoilage. During spoilage, low molecular weight alkaline compounds like ammonia and trimethyl ammonia, generally known as TVB-N, are produced which can lead to off-flavors and subsequent sensory rejection. The TVB-N and pH were measured at different times (0, 12, 24, 48 h). The initial TVB-N value of sardine fish was found to be 11.50 ± 1.25 mg/100 g which increased to 19.72 ± 0.55 mg/100 g after 24 h 27.25 ± 1.75 mg/100 g after 48 h and 36 ± 2.25 mg/100 g after 72 h of storage. As specified by the EC guidelines (Commission Decision 95/149/EC, 1995), for fish samples, 35 mg/100 g fish flesh TVB-N is considered permissible [49]. With an increase in TVB-N values, a subsequent increase in pH has also been reported [46,58]. The initial pH of the fish sample was 6.01and it gradually increased to 7.9 at 72 h of storage. The variation of TVB-N with pH is given in Supplementary data S4. The applicability of both PVA/PRS/CTE films and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films as freshness indicators were studied by fitting them onto transparent packages (Fig. 7). Both the indicator films showed visible colour changes as storage time increased. The colour change of both the films from purple (fresh) to greenish purple (sub fresh) to completely green (spoiled) was visible with naked eyes. The TVB-N released from spoiled fish was absorbed by the films, leading to the production of hydroxyl ions, which in turn contributed to the colour changes of anthocyanin immobilized in the film matrices [35]. A similar colour change was observed when CTE immobilized hydroxypropyl methylcellulose/microcrystalline cellulose bio-composite film was used to monitor the freshness of mackerel [59]. Compared to PVA/PRS/CTE films, PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films showed distinguishing colour changes.

Microbial activity is the main reason for fish spoilage. During spoilage, low molecular weight alkaline compounds like ammonia and trimethyl ammonia, generally known as TVB-N, are produced which can lead to off-flavors and subsequent sensory rejection. The TVB-N and pH were measured at different times (0, 12, 24, 48 h). The initial TVB-N value of sardine fish was found to be 11.50 ± 1.25 mg/100 g which increased to 19.72 ± 0.55 mg/100 g after 24 h 27.25 ± 1.75 mg/100 g after 48 h and 36 ± 2.25 mg/100 g after 72 h of storage. As specified by the EC guidelines (Commission Decision 95/149/EC, 1995), for fish samples, 35 mg/100 g fish flesh TVB-N is considered permissible [49]. With an increase in TVB-N values, a subsequent increase in pH has also been reported [46,58]. The initial pH of the fish sample was 6.01and it gradually increased to 7.9 at 72 h of storage. The variation of TVB-N with pH is given in Supplementary data S4. The applicability of both PVA/PRS/CTE films and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films as freshness indicators were studied by fitting them onto transparent packages (Fig. 7). Both the indicator films showed visible colour changes as storage time increased. The colour change of both the films from purple (fresh) to greenish purple (sub fresh) to completely green (spoiled) was visible with naked eyes. The TVB-N released from spoiled fish was absorbed by the films, leading to the production of hydroxyl ions, which in turn contributed to the colour changes of anthocyanin immobilized in the film matrices [35]. A similar colour change was observed when CTE immobilized hydroxypropyl methylcellulose/microcrystalline cellulose bio-composite film was used to monitor the freshness of mackerel [59]. Compared to PVA/PRS/CTE films, PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films showed distinguishing colour changes.

In this work, colorimetric films of PVA, PRS incorporated with CD, BS and CTE were developed and its application in monitoring the freshness of sardine samples was demonstrated. The presence of PRS, CD, BS and CTE decreased the crystallinity of PVA matrix and the hydrogen bonds formed between PVA and PRS/CD/BS/CTE increased the film's mechanical and barrier properties. Colorimetric films PVA/PRS/CTE and PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE displayed visible and distinguishable colour changes in a range of pH 1–11. The application trial on sardine fish samples revealed the potential of PVA/PRS/CD/BS/CTE films in food packaging for freshness monitoring of aquatic products. Future studies should explore the antibacterial activity of PRS composite films to widen its application in biomedical field.
